front: Novel: told from three alternating narrative voices -- first person (I), second person (you/tu), and third person (he) -- as a dying Mexican Revolution veteran recalls his life
back: The Death of Artemio Cruz (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novel: the title character betrays his revolutionary ideals and becomes a corrupt businessman; he dies on April 10, 1959
back: The Death of Artemio Cruz (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novel: wife Catalina and daughter Teresa sit by a dying tycoon's bedside hoping to learn the location of his will
back: The Death of Artemio Cruz (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novel: secretary Padilla plays tape recordings of a dying tycoon's business deals to keep him conscious
back: The Death of Artemio Cruz (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novel: the title character abandons his cellmate Gonzalo Bernal to be executed by firing squad, then marries Bernal's sister and inherits Don Gamaliel Bernal's estate
back: The Death of Artemio Cruz (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novel: the title character's son Lorenzo is sent to manage estates in Cocuya to spite his mother, and later dies fighting Franco's forces in Spain
back: The Death of Artemio Cruz (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novel: the title character's only childhood friend Lunero was a servant; after accidentally shooting his uncle, the title character fled to Veracruz
back: The Death of Artemio Cruz (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novel: dedicated to American sociologist C. Wright Mills, author of The Power Elite
back: The Death of Artemio Cruz (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novel: Citizen Kane inspired this novel's structure about the deathbed of a corrupt politician
back: The Death of Artemio Cruz (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novella: written entirely in second person; young historian Felipe Montero is hired by elderly widow Consuelo to catalogue her dead husband's memoirs
Novella: the 109-year-old Consuelo conducted experiments with magical herbs; her dead husband's papers reveal reactionary opinions on Republicanism, Boulanger, and Dreyfus
Novella: a rabbit named Saga and a group of cats that the title character tortures live in a rat-infested, perpetually dark house
Novella: the green-eyed title character is revealed to be a magically created double of old widow Consuelo; Montero transforms into General Llorente
Novel: American governess Harriet Winslow goes to Mexico to tutor the Miranda children and develops a relationship with the elderly title character
Novel: the title character shoots a hole through a silver coin to prove his marksmanship and carries a copy of Don Quixote and a Colt .44
Novel: the title character is shot in the back after burning false land-granting documents from the King of Spain; Tomas Arroyo is then executed by Pancho Villa
Novel: soldiers leave a ballroom of mirrors intact after gazing at their reflections; the mirrored ballroom is all that remains of the Miranda estate
Novel: the title character is nicknamed the 'Indiana General' after his Civil War service and is also partly based on William Benton
Novel: the title character's corpse is dug up and shot by a firing squad to prevent a diplomatic incident; Harriet Winslow fights to have him buried in Arlington Cemetery
Novel: Jane Fonda earned a Razzie nomination and Gregory Peck played the title character in the film adaptation
Novel: Harriet's father left his family to live with a Cuban woman during the American invasion of Cuba
Novel: massive 800-page work divided into sections 'Old World,' 'New World,' and 'Next World'; characters are reborn across historical eras during Philip II's building of El Escorial
Novel: characters include the student Ludovico and peasant girl/witch Celestina; the expansive cast also features Don Quixote and Hieronymus Bosch
Novel: opens in a future Paris where the Arc de Triomphe is made of sand and the Eiffel Tower is a zoo; a 90-year-old concierge gives birth to a baby with six toes and a wine-red cross on his back
Novel: The Garden of Earthly Delights appears in a mirror in El Senor's palace, symbolizing this novel's three-part structure
Novel: 1958 debut novel set in post-revolutionary Mexico City; centers on the enigmatic Ixca Cienfuegos and follows business tycoon Federico Robles
back: Where the Air is Clear (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novel: former revolutionary Federico Robles marries Norma Larragoiti; writer Rodrigo Pola is another character in this post-revolutionary Mexico City novel
back: Where the Air is Clear (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novel: takes its title from a line in Alfonso Reyes's Vision de Anahuac
back: Where the Air is Clear (Carlos Fuentes)

front: Novel: narrated by an unborn fetus; Angel and Angeles scheme to conceive a child born exactly on October 12, 1992 -- the 500th anniversary of Columbus's discovery -- to win a government prize
Novel: borrows its structure from Tristram Shandy
Short Story: Filiberto buys a chac-mool (Mesoamerican reclining statue) that comes to life, turns to flesh, floods his home, and kills him; from the collection Burnt Water
Novel: Nietzsche is reincarnated once every year for 24 hours, appears at the Hotel Metropol, and explains eternal recurrence; characters include prostitute Dorian Dolor and Dante Loredano
Author: wrote the introduction to Frida Kahlo's diary
